coredata.dc:title: Bifunctional collagen fiber/carbon quantum dot fluorescent adsorbent for efficient adsorption and detection of Pb2+
coredata.dc:creator: Jing, Limin
Ding, Qijun
Li, Xia
Lou, Jiang
Liu, Zhuqing
Jiang, Yifei
Han, Wenjia
Cheng, Zheng
coredata.dc:description: In this work, fluorescent adsorbents that can efficiently detect and remove Pb2+ were developed by integrating the designed amino-modified carbon quantum dots and carboxyl-modified collagen. The adsorption properties of the fluorescent adsorbent were further optimized and analyzed using a series of response surface experiments. The maximum adsorption concentration for Pb2+ was 183 mg.g−1. The adsorption isotherms fit well with the Langmuir model, and the adsorption kinetics fit with the pseudo-second-order model. The emission intensity of the fluorescent adsorbent gradually decreased with the increase of the concentration of Pb2+, and had a good linear correlation. In addition, the mechanism of detection and removal of Pb2+ by fluorescent adsorbents was further demonstrated. The novel three-dimensional structured fluorescent aerogel can be used as a promising adsorbent with good adsorption concentration and sensing ability for Pb2+, which shows great prospects in wastewater.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent aerogel
Efficient detection and removal
Pb2+
Collagen fiber
Carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Mechanism of adsorption and sensing of Pb2+

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the National Key Research and development Program of China ( 2020YFC 1910301 ); Shandong Academy of Sciences , Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province , China ( ZR2021QC158 ). Innovation Pilot Project of Integration of Science, Education and Industry of Shandong Province ( 2020KJC-ZD06 ); Foundation of State Key Laboratory of Biobased Material and Green Papermaking , Qilu University of Technology ( ZZ20200107 , ZZ20200118 & ZZ20190111 ; GZKF202022 ); Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province, China ( ZR2020QB002 ); Outstanding Youth Innovation Team Project of Shandong Provincial University ( 242007040109 ); National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22001140 ); Shandong Academy of Sciences , National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22108086 ), Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation ( 2022A1515010323 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) PL excitation and emission spectra of CDs (λem = 400 nm, λex = 350 nm); (b) UV–vis spectra of CDs solution; (c) Fluorescence lifetime of CDs; (d) Emission spectra of CDs excited by different wavelengths; (e) Comparison of fluorescence intensity of CDs under different dilution ratios; (f) Comparison of fluorescence intensity of CDs under different metal ions (g) Fluorescence intensity of CDs at different pH values; (h) FT-IR spectra of CDs (i). Wide-scan XPS spectra of CDs (red curve), inset split-peak spectra of element N (yellow curve). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(a) Stacked images of the prepared aerogel under laser confocal microscope; (b) N-element mapping spectrum of aerogel; (c, d) Micro-CT of fluorescent aerogels.

3D surface and 2D plan of response surface between (a) initial metal ion concentration and temperature of adsorption environment; (b) pH and the temperature of adsorption environment; (c) pH and initial metal ion concentration, the corresponding two-dimensional relationship are below.

(a) Adsorption equilibrium isotherms fitted by Langmuir model respectively; (b) Adsorption data were fitted via the pseudo- first-order model and (c) the pseudo-second-order model; (d) Linear curve of the fluorescence intensity of the prepared adsorbent changing with the concentration of pb2+ ion; (e) Recycling efficiency of fluorescent aerogels; (f) Comparison of adsorption concentration of original aerogels and fluorescent aerogels.

(a) High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s in aerogels; (b) High-resolution XPS spectra of N1s in aerogels; (c) High-resolution XPS spectra of O1s in aerogels; (d) High-resolution XPS spectra of Pb4f in aerogels adsorbed with Pb2+; (e) Elemental SEM mapping of N that was adsorbed on aerogel; (f) Elemental SEM mapping of Pb that was adsorbed on aerogel.
Maximum adsorption concentration for Pb2+ compared to previous work with biomass adsorbents.

head.ce:title: Bifunctional collagen fiber/carbon quantum dot fluorescent adsorbent for efficient adsorption and detection of Pb2+

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Biobased Material and Green Papermaking, Qilu University of Technology, Shandong Academy of Sciences, Jinan 250353, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Biobased Material and Green Papermaking

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Biobased Material and Green Papermaking, Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences), Jinan, 250353, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Pulp and Paper Engineering, Plant Fiber Material Science Research Center, School of Light Industry and Engineering, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou 510640, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Pulp and Paper Engineering
Plant Fiber Material Science Research Center
School of Light Industry and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Pulp and Paper Engineering, Plant Fiber Material Science Research Center, School of Light Industry and Engineering, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou, 510640, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, fluorescent adsorbents that can efficiently detect and remove Pb2+ were developed by integrating the designed amino-modified carbon quantum dots and carboxyl-modified collagen. The adsorption properties of the fluorescent adsorbent were further optimized and analyzed using a series of response surface experiments. The maximum adsorption concentration for Pb2+ was 183 mg.g−1. The adsorption isotherms fit well with the Langmuir model, and the adsorption kinetics fit with the pseudo-second-order model. The emission intensity of the fluorescent adsorbent gradually decreased with the increase of the concentration of Pb2+, and had a good linear correlation. In addition, the mechanism of detection and removal of Pb2+ by fluorescent adsorbents was further demonstrated. The novel three-dimensional structured fluorescent aerogel can be used as a promising adsorbent with good adsorption concentration and sensing ability for Pb2+, which shows great prospects in wastewater.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Pollution of wastewater by toxic heavy metal ions is a worldwide environmental issue that has attracted much attention (Ali, 2010; Carolin et al., 2017; Kumar and Chawla, 2014). Heavy metal ions are not biodegraded and tend to accumulate in organisms and eventually enter the food chain (Akpor and Muchie, 2010; Giani et al., 2021; Zhang and Wang, 2020). Lead (Pb) is an important pollutant in wastewater mainly from untreated municipal waste, over-fertilized soil and deposition of industrial waste gas (Cheng and Hu, 2010; Khan et al., 2021). It has been reported that countries around the world have released about 800,000 tons of Pb2+ into water and soil annually over the past few decades. Undegradable Pb2+ in water will not only harm the ecosystem but also seriously threaten human health and safety through human contact and the amplification effect of the food chain. (Chibowska et al., 2016; Kim et al., 2014; Metryka et al., 2018). Therefore, it is of great significance to develop a technology for rapid and efficient detection and removal of Pb2+ in wastewater.

The common treatment methods for lead-containing wastewater mainly include chemical precipitation, ion exchange (Bozecka et al., 2016; Stephenson and Dryfe, 2007), flocculation (Ponou et al., 2014; Zhang and Chen, 2015), Phyto ecological remediation (He et al., 2019; Nikulina et al., 2021), and adsorption (Carolin et al., 2017). Adsorption is the most widely used method, and the preparation of an efficient Pb2+ adsorbent is a very meaningful research topic. (Chen et al., 2018; Liu, 2015; Perez-Chavez et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2010). In recent years, many scholars had studied various inexpensive biomass materials and prepared adsorbent materials for wastewater treatment through pretreatment and cross-linking modification, such as chitosan-based adsorbents (Briao et al., 2020), fruit shells (Hossain et al., 2012; Popuri et al., 2007; Sousa et al., 2009), cellulose-based adsorbents (Arumughan et al., 2021; Lin et al., 2015; Varghese et al., 2019), etc. Zhao et al. synthesized a microporous silk fibroin bead adsorbent with tentacle-like functional ligands, which exhibited good removal rates for various metal ions (Xiao et al., 2016a; Zhao et al., 2021). Cui et al. used a chitosan derivative adsorbent for the removal of Pb2+ from an aqueous solution and the adsorption concentration of the prepared adsorbent reached 143.3 mg.g−1 (Cui et al., 2013). However, new adsorbents should have the characteristics of biodegradability, simple and environmentally friendly preparation methods, high recycling times and multiple functions. Collagen fiber is a macromolecular hydrophilic colloid with low price and regeneration mainly from animal skin, bone and tanning industry waste. It not only provides a natural framework for the formation of three-dimensional network structure, but also provides a large number of surface-active groups, which are beneficial conducive to the aggregation of heavy metal ions. Therefore, collagen fiber is a promising candidate for the synthesis of excellent adsorbents. Meanwhile, carbon quantum dots (CDs) are very promising nanomaterials in the field of smart sensing due to their excellent fluorescence intensity, chemical stability, and nontoxicity. In addition, the amino-modified CDs can be easily cross-linked with gelatin without chemical additives and can provide more adsorption sites to chelate metal ions. The composite aerogels prepared with collagen fiber as the matrix and CDs as the fluorescence source may realize the dual function of bio-based adsorbents for adsorption and detection of heavy metal ions.

In this study, a simple, environmentally friendly, three-dimensional mesh fluorescent aerogel with dual functions was designed and developed using CDs and collagen fibers to simultaneously adsorb and detect Pb2+.The adsorption behavior of fluorescent adsorbents on Pb2+ was systematically studied by response surface methodology, including the effects of initial Pb2+ concentration and contact time on the adsorption concentration and removal rate. The adsorption kinetics, adsorption isotherm, adsorption and detection mechanism were comprehensively investigated. The experimental results show that the fluorescent adsorbent can achieve sensitive fluorescence sensing and efficient adsorption. Bifunctional fluorescent aerogels have great potential for the removal and detection of metal ions in wastewater.

The collagen fiber (CF, ≥99 %, type I, 115–130 kDa) was provided by Shengquan Group Co., Ltd. (Jinan, China). Polyethyleneimine (PEI, 50 wt%, 50 % in water, Molecular Weight (Mw): 25000) was purchased from Macklin Chemical (shanghai, China), The citric acid (AR) were obtained from Aladdin Chemical. Bromo succinic acid (≥96 %, Powder, Mw: 196.98) was supplied by Macklin Chemical (Shanghai, China). All reagents in this work do not need further purification.

The citric acid (0.4 g) and polyethyleneimine (2.835 g) were dissolved in 120 mL of ultra-pure water under stirring. The hydrothermal reaction was carried out under the conditions of continuous reaction at 180 °C for about 20 h. After the reaction, the product was placed in a dialysis bag for separation and purification. During the dialysis process, ultra-pure water is used for dialysis every 8 h for about three days to fully remove unreacted molecular precursors.

Collagen was modified by using Bromo succinic acid. First, one gram of absolutely dry collagen fiber was fully mixed with two grams of Bromo succinic acid, and the pH was adjusted to 10. Then, the product was fully washed with deionized water to neutral. The carboxylated collagen fiber was refrigerated for use.

Fluorescent adsorbent were prepared via the Schiff-base reaction of amino and aldehyde groups(Zhang et al., 2022). Firstly, one gram of absolutely dry collagen fiber was adjusted to acidic pH, and continuous stirring at room temperature. The carboxylated collagen fiber was fully mixed with CDs. Glutaraldehyde was added for cross-linking reaction, in which the original unmodified collagen fiber was the control group. Finally, The product was freeze-drying for about 72 h to obtained a series of fluorescent aerogels.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (ALPHA,Germany) recorded the infrared spectra of CF and aerogels. Detailed, the CDs and C-CF were tested under the ATR probe. The fluorescence properties of fluorescent substances were measured by fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-7100, Japan). The fluorescence life-time of CDs was measured using a steady-state transient fluorescence spectrometer (C9920-02G, Japan). The three-dimensional image inside the aerogel material was obtained by the multi-scale X-ray nano-computed tomography (micro-CT) system (Skyscan 2211, Germany). The chemical compositions of the CDs and adsorbed fluorescent aerogels were studied by XPS (ESCALABXi+, UK). Laser confocal scanning microscope (CLSM) images are taken with Leica TCS SP8 CARS (Germany) at x64 magnification. In order to analyze the thermal stability of fluorescent aerogel, thermogravimetric analyzer was used (TGA-Q50, USA). The pH meters was used to detect the acidity of adsorption systems(PHS-3E, China). Aerogels were prepared by a vertical freeze-drying machine (Labconco, USA) The concentrations of different metal ions were determined by atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS, GGX-600, China).

The experiment was repeated three times to obtain the average value and relative error. Isotherm experiments were performed at pH 6.5 for 24 h at different metal ion concentrations. Kinetic analysis was also performed based on the adsorption time at a fixed initial concentration (100 mg/L). All experiments were performed in triplicate and the adsorption concentration (qe) of the aerogel was calculated using Eq. (1). The desorption of metal ions from aerogels was studied using HCl as a regenerator. Also, after each metal ion adsorption/desorption cycle, the aerogels were rinsed three times with water to remove excess HCl solution.

As shown in the graphical abstract, Collagen fiber was modified by carboxylation with Bromo succinic acid as the modifier. The carbon quantum dots were obtained by a simple hydrothermal method using citric acid as the carbon source and PEI as the nitrogen source. The as-prepared hydrogel was employed to adsorb the heavy metal ions. In addition, the fluorescent aerogels were synthesized using the Schiff base reaction. The aerogel has a three-dimensional network structure. In the synthesis process, C-CF acts as the backbone of aerogel, which is not only connected to CDs but also can form a three-dimensional mesh structure. Among them, CDs can induce metal ion aggregation. Because the existence of CDs, aerogels can visually detect metal ions.

The emission wavelength was at 495 nm, and the excitation wavelength was at 305 nm (Fig. 1a). The photoluminescence (PL) properties of the prepared adsorbent under UV conditions were observed. As shown by the UV–vis absorption spectrum, the absorption peak of the synthetic fluorescent adsorbent was about 365 nm. This phenomenon analysis was caused by the n- π * transition of CDs(Xie et al., 2019). The average fluorescence lifetime of the prepared CDs was 8.53 ns (Fig. 1c). At the same time, we collected PL spectra at diverse excitations from 310 to 400 nm (Fig. 1d). It can be seen that the peak position does not change with the excitation wavelength. This behavior was due to various oxygen-containing functional groups in CDs, which contain many localized energy levels and can realized energy level transition (Das et al., 2018). CDs have an aggregation burst effect due to their unique nanoscale (Fig. S1), with optimal fluorescence performance at a concentration of 7 ppm as shown in Fig. 1e. As shown in Fig. 1f, the carbon quantum dots all have a certain fluorescence burst effects on metal ions, among which the burst effect on Pb2+ is the best. This competitive advantage became more and more obvious as the Pb2+ concentration increased. CDs have better pH stability due to their unique nanostructures (Fig. 1g and Fig. S2). As shown in Fig. 1i, one peak at 399.7 eV, appeared, which were attributed to NH, respectively. The abundant amine enhances the collection and adsorption of metal ions by aerogels, allowing CDs to be used not only as fluorescent sensors, but also to improve the capture of metal ions by aerogels (Guo et al., 2019; Luo et al., 2021).

The unique three-dimensional structure of aerogels provides channels for the aggregation of metal solutions. The microscopic diagram of fluorescent aerogel was shown in Fig. 2 . The fibrous network structure can be seen clearly. At the same time, as shown in Fig. 2b, the uniform distribution of N elements can be seen. This also proves the successful grafting of CDs. The image of Micro-CT can help to prove the structural characteristics of aerogel. The fluorescent aerogels have abundant three-dimensional cross-linked networks which provide more active sites for the contact of metal ions with the internal functional groups. The cross-linked network of the prepared aerogels could be seen by the micro-CT images, and this result corresponds to the SEM images (Fig. 2b). The images in Fig. 2a and Fig. 2b showed the uniform distribution and stable cross-linking of CDs in the aerogel network, while the abundant -NH2 − on the surface of fluorescent substance also increase the chelating effect of the aerogel on metal ions. All synthesized fluorescent aerogels were shown in Fig. 2c and d, showing abundant porous structures. At the same time, as shown in Fig. S4, the fluorescent aerogels have good thermal stability in wastewater and can be used normally.

The pH value of the aqueous solution will affect the protonation or deprotonation of the functional groups on the surface of the adsorbent, and affect the existing valence state of metal ions. Therefore, the pH value of the adsorption environment is an experimental parameter worthy of attention.(Liu et al., 2021). The pH in the following experiments was set to be <7.0 to avoid the formation of metal hydroxide precipitate of Pb2+ at a higher pH. The results from the response surface series of experiments, as shown in Fig. 3 , indicated that the apparent adsorption concentration increases with increasing pH. It also has a good apparent equilibrium adsorption concentration at pH = 6.5. This is mainly because since the aerogel we prepared contains both amino and carboxyl groups (Xue et al., 2021). The results showed that the ionization equilibrium point of amino and carboxyl groups was pH = 6.5, which was favorable to capture metal ions under this condition. When it was lower than this value, Pb2+ would compete with H+ for the binding sites on the adsorbent surface, and the ion band positive sites should repel each other, at which time H+ would occupy the adsorption sites on the adsorbent surface that should belong to Pb2+, and the total number of adsorption sites was constant, so it was going to cause the adsorption concentration of the adsorbent for metal ions to become weaker. With the increase of pH, the degree of protonation on the adsorbent surface would be weakened, the competition ability of H+ would become poor, and the adsorption amount was going to increase due to the complexation of metal ions with the adsorbent surface.

The effect of the initial metal solution concentration on the adsorption process is mainly reflected in the increase of the mass transfer driving force of the reaction. As shown in Fig. 3, qe increases with increasing concentration, which proved that the adsorption reaction was promoted by increasing the mass transfer driving force. However, with increasing concentration, the removal of metal ions by aerogel decreases with the same amount of adsorbent. The role of temperature on the reaction is likewise not negligible. As shown in Table S1, the calculation of the Gibbs free energy showed that ΔG° was all less than zero. The series of experiments shown in Fig. 3 and Fig. S5 showed that the equilibrium adsorption concentration raises with the increase in temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of adsorption and sensing of Pb2+
ce:section.ce:para: Langmuir and Freundlich have been used to fit the experimental data respectively to study the adsorption mechanism. The corresponding calculation parameters were shown in Table S2. The correlation coefficient (R2) shows that the experimental data fit well with the Langmuir model (0.99). The adsorption isotherms of aerogels conform to the Langmuir, model implying that the adsorption process consists of a combination of physical and chemical adsorption. Adsorption kinetics of aerogels conform to a pseudo-second kinetic model. A high correlation coefficient indicates that the adsorption process of this adsorbent on the adsorbent is mainly controlled by chemisorption(Xue et al., 2021). However, in practical applications, although the R2 of the proposed secondary kinetic model can reach above 0.97, the physical diffusion process of the adsorbent cannot be ignored.

The detection of Pb2+ concentration by three-dimensional porous fluorescent aerogel was further investigated. As shown in Fig. 4d, the fluorescence intensity of the aerogel varied with the metal ion concentration from 10 to 1000 mg/L in the range of decreasing. In addition, a good linear correlation was found between the fluorescence intensity of this new three-dimensional porous fluorescent aerogel and the metal ion concentration. Three-dimensional porous fluorescent aerogels exhibited good adsorption and stability. The fluorescence-modified adsorbent has better adsorption concentration compared with the original aerogel (Fig. 4f). This was mainly because the addition of CDs brings more amino groups, which improves the chelation of metal ions and further increases the adsorption concentration (Table S3). Further FT-IR and XPS spectra were used to investigate the sensing and adsorption mechanism on Pb2+. Fig. S6 showed the FT-IR spectra before and after the adsorption of metal ions. After Pb2+ sorption, the broad and strong band centered at 3390 cm−1 (the stretching vibration of the NH and OH groups) decreased, the symmetrical stretching vibration of -COO at 1403 cm−1 were weakened. And the band at 1667 cm−1 (the stretching vibration of CON-H) shifted obviously. At the same time, as shown in Table 1 , compared with other biological adsorbents, the adsorption concentration was better These results indicated that amino, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups of the synthesized fluorescent aerogels played a role in the Pb2+ sorption process.

The interaction between metal ions and adsorbents was clarified by peak fitting of XPS data. Fig. S7 showed a typical wide-scan XPS spectrum of a fluorescent aerogel after adsorption, where the energy peak of Pb 4f can be seen. Comparison of the C1s spectra of the post-adsorption aerogels revealed no significant change in the C1s spectra of the aerogels after adsorption of metal ions (Fig. 5a), indicating no significant effect of adsorption of these C-containing functional group ions. In the high-resolution spectra of N 1 s, the peaks corresponding to -N-H (−NH2) at the 399.5 eV. and -N-C (-CONH) at 400.5 eV increased to 399.7 eV and 401.3 eV, individually (Fig. 5c). The phenomenon of increased binding energy confirms that -NH2 and -CONH contribute to form complexes associated with adsorbate (Li et al., 2018). This is because the the amino group is deprotonated, increasing the binding energy. It is also evident from the high-resolution spectra of O1 in aerogels that the peak shifts to 531.30 eV and 532.40 eV after Pb2+ adsorption (Fig. 5c). Two peaks are observed due to the presence of O groups (CO and C-OH). The peak at about 139 eV in the simultaneous wide-scan XPS spectrum (Fig. S3) corresponds to Pb 4f, which confirms the distribution of Pb2+ on the adsorbent surface. Similarly, the distribution of Pb and N elements on the adsorbent surface can be seen by SEM observation of the elemental distribution of the fluorescent adsorbent after adsorption as in Fig. 5f and Fig. 5e(Fig. S8). The latter high-resolution XPS spectrum of the Pb 4f analysis shows strong peaks at 143.7 eV and 139.1 eV attributed to Pb 4f5/2 and Pb 4f7/2, correspondingly (Fig. 5d). 139.1 eV of the Pb 4f7/2 peak can be attributed to -COOPb+, (-COO)2Pb and -OPb+(Liang et al., 2020). In summary, the effective functional groups on the surface of fluorescent aerogels such as carboxyl and amino groups of fluorescent aerogels function as electron donors for the adsorption of Pb2+.

In summary, in this study, aerogels with real-time visual response were developed by modifying collagen fibers through carboxylation. The aerogel as an adsorbent has a three-dimensional network structure, which provides more active sites for adsorption. Also, the uniform distribution of CDs on the aerogel and the abundant amino groups on the surface increase the aggregation and chelation of metal ions, making this adsorbent with dual functions of adsorption and detection. Overall, this fluorescent aerogel adsorbent prepared shows a versatile strategy for the adsorption and detection of metal ions in wastewater. As an emerging force in the field of bio-based adsorbent materials, this may encourage the development of green bifunctional adsorbents.

Xia Li: Conceptualization, Methodology. Zhuqing Liu: Software. Loujiang: Data curation. Writing- Original draft preparation. Yifei Jiang: Visualization, Investigation. Zheng Cheng: Methodology, Reviewing. Qijun Ding and Wenjia Han: Supervision. Limin Jing: Writing- Original draft preparation, Writing- Reviewing and Editing. All authors had drafted, revised the work and approved the final paper.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Key Research and development Program of China (2020YFC 1910301); Shandong Academy of Sciences, Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province, China (ZR2021QC158). Innovation Pilot Project of Integration of Science, Education and Industry of Shandong Province (2020KJC-ZD06); Foundation of State Key Laboratory of Biobased Material and Green Papermaking, Qilu University of Technology (ZZ20200107, ZZ20200118 & ZZ20190111; GZKF202022); Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province, China (ZR2020QB002); Outstanding Youth Innovation Team Project of Shandong Provincial University (242007040109); National Natural Science Foundation of China (22001140); Shandong Academy of Sciences, National Natural Science Foundation of China (22108086), Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (2022A1515010323).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2023.161989.
